nativeRange.habitat: Slower-flowing tributaries and standing water bodies such as swamps and irrigation channels with dense aquatic or riparian vegetation
nativeRange.biotope: Subtropical lowland freshwater; often found near substrate in schools

diet.notes: Readily accepts quality flake and pellet foods. Has been documented eating small live and frozen foods such as bloodworms, daphnia, and brine shrimp, as well as plant-based foods including algae wafers. Appreciates dietary variety.

imageCandidates.notes: Derek Ramsey, 1800x867. Shoal of several vivid golden individuals with metallic gold flanks, dark speckling and orange-red fins - best represents this schooling species in the hobby.
Andre Karwath, 2000x1007. Tack-sharp lone male in full nuptial colour - golden body, red eye, dark scale speckling, black caudal blotch and bright orange-red fins.
Chmee2, 4752x3168. Crisp high-res single specimen against a dark background showing golden-green iridescent flank, dark speckling and red fins.

summary: The Gold Barb is a selectively bred ornamental form of Barbodes semifasciolatus, prized for its vivid golden coloration and cool-water tolerance. Generally peaceful and easy to keep, it is well suited to community aquariums and temperate or subtropical setups.
careNotes:
The Gold Barb is the selectively bred ornamental form of Barbodes semifasciolatus, first developed in the 1960s. It is commonly sold in the aquarium trade under the name 'Puntius schuberti', an informal label coined after Thomas Schubert, who popularised the strain, rather than a valid scientific name. The wild form carries green-silver coloration with faint banding and is rarely encountered in the hobby. An albino variant also exists.

The Gold Barb's tolerance of cooler water — down to around 16°C — makes it compatible with goldfish-temperature aquariums and temperate community setups, and no heater is needed in most temperate homes. It tends to do best in the 18–24°C range.

Gold Barbs are a schooling species and generally do best in groups of six or more. Smaller groups can lead to skittish behaviour and increased squabbling among tankmates. As an active mid-water swimmer, this species benefits from a tank that provides open swimming space alongside planted areas for cover. A footprint of at least 90 cm in length tends to matter more than raw volume for this active swimmer. Commercially produced specimens have sometimes been noted to show reduced fin size or minor deformities associated with inbreeding, so sourcing from reputable suppliers is advisable.

Gold Barbs are generally considered less prone to fin-nipping than Tiger Barbs, while being somewhat more active than the placid Cherry Barb — making them a reasonable middle-ground choice for mixed community aquariums.
breedingNotes: The Gold Barb is an egg-scattering free spawner that provides no parental care, and breeding is generally considered moderate in difficulty. Conditioning adults on live and frozen foods, combined with a gradual temperature increase toward the upper end of the range (around 22–24°C), tends to trigger spawning. A separate breeding tank is often recommended, with a mesh or marble base layer to let the eggs fall out of reach and reduce predation by the adults. Eggs typically hatch within 24–48 hours, and fry become free-swimming within another day or so. They can be started on infusoria or commercial liquid fry foods before progressing to baby brine shrimp as they grow.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (7.6 cm max), temperature (18-24°C), pH (6.0-8.0), hardness (dH 5-19), native range, and depth range. Minor disagreement on temperature minimum (FishBase 18°C vs SeriouslyFish 16°C); averaged toward SeriouslyFish's lower value given known cool-water tolerance documented across multiple hobby sources.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3 in / 7.6 cm), temperature (64-75°F = 17.8-23.9°C), pH (6.0-8.0), tank size (20 gal / 76L for 5-6 fish), lifespan (5-7 years), and temperament (peaceful). Tank volume slightly lower than SeriouslyFish footprint guidance; final minVolumeLiters set to 100L as cautious round number suitable for a school of 6+ active swimmers.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Confirms adult size (3 in / 7.6 cm) and 29-gallon (110L) minimum for a school, consistent with more conservative tank sizing. Confirms omnivorous diet and schooling active behaviour.
